chunks:
I  started  my  ML  in  the  second  year  of  college.  Due  to  a  lack  of  resources,  I  went  through  
courses
 
on
 
YouTube
 
and
 
built
 
small
 
projects
 
like
 
a
 
movie
 
recommendation
 
system,
 
IPL
 
score
 
prediction,
 
etc.
 
Then,
 
when
 
I
 
started
 
my
 
journey
 
as
 
a
 
Quantum
 
ML
 
intern,
 
I
 
started
 
to
 
know
 
that
 
there
 
are
 
other
 
different
 
methods
 
like
 
SVM
 
(which
 
can
 
be
 
implemented
 
using
 
Quantum
 
ML.
 
So
 
then
 
I
 
started
 
to
 
search
 
more
 
in
 
this
 
ML
 
field
 
and
 
got
 
to
 
know
 
about
 
more
 
models.
 
While
 
reading
 
different
 
research
 
papers,
 
I
 
found
 
it
 
interesting:
 
why
 
can't
 
we
 
predict
 
the
 
most
 
probable
 
places
 
where
 
we
 
can
 
find
 
archaeological
 
sites?
 
As
 
a
 
resident
 
of
 
India,
 
we
 
have
 
a
 
great
 
history
 
which
 
has
 
been
 
recorded,
 
like
 
the
 
Ramayana,
 
Mahabharata,
 
etc.
 
So
 
why
 
can't
 
I
 
pick
 
the
 
places
 
and
 
the
 
suitable
 
conditions
 
where
 
we
 
can
 
find
 
an
 
old
 
civilization?
 
Then
 
I
 
started
 
my
 
project
 
EpicGeo.
  I  built  a  pipeline  that  extracts  geographic  information  from  Sanskrit  epics—NER  to  find  place  
mentions,
 
geocoding
 
to
 
locate
 
them,
 
some
 
uncertainty
 
modelling
 
(since
 
"seven
 
days
 
east"
 
obviously
 
isn't
 
a
 
coordinate),
 
and
 
rasterisation
 
to
 
get
 
everything
 
into
 
a
 
format
 
a
 
model
 
can
 
use.
 
Then
 
I
 
put
 
together
 
nine
 
feature
 
datasets:
 
geology,
 
land
 
use,
 
river
 
proximity,
 
reconstructed
 
ancient
 
routes,
 
and
 
a
 
co-mention
 
graph
 
of
 
which
 
places
 
show
 
up
 
together
 
across
 
texts.
 
A
 
Random
 
Forest
 
does
 
the
 
actual
 
classification,
 
trained
 
against
 
known
 
Indian
 
sites.
  Honestly,  picking  the  model  is  where  I  noticed  my  biggest  gap.  I  went  from  thinking  
regression,
 
to
 
decision
 
trees,
 
to
 
random
 
forests—mostly
 
by
 
trying
 
things
 
and
 
seeing
 
what
 
stuck.
 
Looking
 
back,
 
if
 
my
 
foundations
 
had
 
been
 
solid
 
from
 
the
 
start,
 
I
 
probably
 
could
 
have
 
reasoned
 
my
 
way
 
straight
 
to
 
a
 
random
 
forest,
 
or
 
maybe
 
something
 
better,
 
instead
 
of
 
stumbling
 
into
 
it.
 
It's
 
the
 
same
 
story
 
in
 
other
 
parts
 
of
 
the
 
project.
 
My
 
uncertainty
 
modelling
 
was
 
more
 
improvised
 
than
 
principled,
 
and
 
spatial
 
data
 
has
 
issues
 
like
 
autocorrelation
 
and
 
leakage
 
between
 
nearby
 
points
 
that
 
I've
 
only
 
patched
 
up
 
informally.
 
I
 
want
 
to
 
get
 
to
 
a
 
point
 
where
 
my
 
choices
 
come
 
from
 
actually
 
understanding
 
the
 
theory,
 
not
 
just
 
habit.
  That's  what  I'm  hoping  to  get  out  of  MLSS.  I've  already  shown  I  can  take  a  hard  problem  and  
get
 
it
 
working
 
on
 
my
 
own—what
 
I'm
 
missing
 
is
 
the
 
rigorous
 
foundation
 
that
 
turns
 
a
 
working
 
pipeline
 
into
 
real
 
research,
 
and
 
people
 
who'll
 
push
 
back
 
on
 
my
 
reasoning
 
instead
 
of
 
just
 
nodding
 
along.
  I  don't  think  the  best  fit  for  a  program  like  this  is  whoever  already  knows  the  most.  I  think  it's  
someone
 
with
 
a
 
real
 
problem
 
in
 
hand,
 
who's
 
already
 
gotten
 
it
 
partly
 
working,
 
and
 
who
 
has
 
specific
 
gaps
 
they
 
actually
 
want
 
to
 
close.
 
That
 
feels
 
like
 
me.
